ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for beef tendon with chilli sate sauce. FOODON_03302051 (beef food product) used as closest parent. A more specific term for beef tendon preparations is absent from FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Bovine meat and meat products) used as closest parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for beef tendon with sate chilli sauce as a prepared dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 (Beef product) used as closest parent. No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for beef tendon with chilli sate sauce.

serving.description: 1 portion as served in a Singapore restaurant
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of beef tendon with chilli sate sauce in Singapore is approximately 200 g, comprising braised beef tendon pieces coated in a rich, spiced sate chilli sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, variety meats and by-products, feet, cooked, simmered (USDA SR Legacy) and Sate sauce, peanut-based (USDA FoodData Central) — composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for beef tendon with sate chilli sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked beef tendon/feet (FDC ID 173793, 'Beef, variety meats and by-products, feet, cooked, simmered') with USDA FoodData Central data for peanut-based sate/satay sauce (FDC ID 2003573) in an approximate 70:30 ratio by weight. Energy and macros are cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for similar braised beef offal dishes. Values represent a best-estimate composite and carry medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed meat-and-sauce dish with peanut-based sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef tendon with sate chilli sauce. GI estimated at 32 based on analogous low-GI mixed meat dishes with peanut-based sauces from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Beef tendon itself contributes negligible glycemic load; the sate sauce contributes modest carbohydrate from peanuts and sugar with a low-to-medium GI. Composite GI of 32 (low category) is defensible. GL per 200 g serving = (32 × 5.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 3.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1040 mg per 200 g serving) from fish sauce, sate sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content, which can approach 45% of the recommended daily sodium limit (2300 mg) in a single serving; request reduced-sauce or sauce-on-side preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~32) and modest carbohydrate content (~5.2 g per 100 g) with high protein from beef tendon
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI and low carbohydrate content make this dish relatively suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; however, the sugar content in the sate sauce should be monitored, and portion size should be controlled to limit total carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~3.6 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (~72 mg per 100 g) from beef tendon, combined with high sodium from sate sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation due to its saturated fat, cholesterol, and high sodium content; the peanut-derived unsaturated fats in the sate sauce provide some cardiovascular benefit but do not offset the overall sodium and saturated fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Tendon with Chilli Sauce (Gan Bo Xao Sate) is a Singapore restaurant dish with Vietnamese culinary roots, featuring slow-braised beef tendon stir-fried in a spiced sate chilli sauce. Beef tendon is a collagen-rich connective tissue cut that becomes gelatinous and tender after prolonged braising, yielding a soft, slightly chewy texture with a rich mouthfeel. The sate chilli sauce — a Southeast Asian condiment made from dried chillies, lemongrass, shallots, garlic, peanuts, fish sauce, and sugar — imparts a complex flavour profile that is simultaneously spicy, savoury, slightly sweet, and aromatic. The dish is typically served as a main course or side dish in Vietnamese-Singaporean restaurants, often accompanied by steamed rice or rice noodles. Nutritionally, beef tendon is high in protein and collagen (gelatin), low in fat compared to other beef cuts, and provides meaningful amounts of zinc, vitamin B12, and iron. The sate sauce contributes additional fat (primarily from peanuts), sodium, and modest carbohydrate. The overall dish is moderate in energy (~195 kcal per 100 g), high in protein (~17.5 g per 100 g), and carries a low glycemic index (~32) due to its minimal carbohydrate content and the peanut-based sauce. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g), which is elevated due to fish sauce and sate seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for those with peanut or fish allergies. It is not halal-certified in most Singapore restaurants due to the use of beef in a non-halal context and fish sauce of uncertain provenance.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for beef tendon with sate chilli sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked beef tendon/feet and USDA FoodData Central data for peanut-based sate sauce in an approximate 70:30 composite ratio. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar low-GI mixed meat-and-peanut-sauce dishes. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct primary source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy (beef feet/tendon, cooked, simmered) and USDA FoodData Central (peanut-based sate sauce) in a 70:30 weight ratio. No direct HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific dish. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for beef tendon/feet and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 3. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for beef tendon with sate chilli sauce was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 4. Halal status is marked false as the dish is served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant and the halal certification status of the establishment and fish sauce provenance cannot be confirmed from available sources. 5. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' (as in the input seed record) to 'Meat and alternatives' as beef tendon is the primary ingredient and the dish functions as a protein-based main or side course, not a condiment. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the composite USDA source data and carry medium confidence.
